name: Greek Moussaka
description: Layered casserole of eggplant, spiced lamb mince, and creamy bechamel sauce baked until golden.
instructions: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, salt and rest 30 minutes, pat dry. Brush with oil and grill until golden.
Brown lamb with diced onion and garlic. Add tomatoes, cinnamon, oregano, salt; simmer 20 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, add flour, cook 1 minute. Slowly whisk in milk until thick. Off heat, stir in egg yolk, nutmeg, and parmesan.
Layer a baking dish: eggplant, meat sauce, eggplant, meat sauce.
Pour bechamel over the top, smooth evenly.
Bake at 180C for 40 minutes until golden and bubbling. Rest 15 minutes before cutting.
tips: Resting the moussaka after baking allows it to set and slice cleanly.
Salting the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato and bell pepper sauce with cumin, paprika, and feta.
instructions: Heat oil in a skillet, sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, cumin, paprika, and chili flakes; cook 30 seconds until fragrant.
Pour in canned tomatoes, crush with a spoon, season with salt. Simmer 10 minutes until thick.
Make 4 wells in the sauce, crack an egg into each.
Cover and cook 5-7 minutes until whites are set but yolks are still runny.
Crumble feta on top, garnish with fresh herbs, serve with crusty bread.
tips: The sauce should be thick before adding eggs — watery sauce makes runny shakshuka.
Cover the pan to steam the egg whites while keeping yolks runny.

name: Falafel
description: Crispy fried chickpea fritters with herbs and spices, served in pita with tahini sauce.
instructions: Drain soaked chickpeas (do NOT use canned). Process with onion, garlic, herbs, cumin, coriander, and salt until a coarse paste forms.
Stir in baking powder. Refrigerate 1 hour for easier shaping.
Shape into balls or patties about 4cm across.
Deep fry at 180C for 3-4 minutes until deeply golden and crispy.
Make tahini sauce: thin tahini with water and lemon juice, season with salt.
Serve in warm pita with tahini, pickled turnips, and salad.
tips: Must use dried chickpeas soaked overnight — canned chickpeas are too wet and fall apart.
The mixture should hold its shape when squeezed — if too wet, add a tablespoon of flour.

name: Hummus
description: Smooth and creamy chickpea dip blended with tahini, lemon, garlic, and olive oil.
instructions: Drain and rinse chickpeas. For extra-smooth hummus, rub chickpeas to remove skins.
Process tahini with lemon juice and garlic for 1 minute until thick and pale.
Add chickpeas, cumin, and salt; process 2 minutes.
With processor running, drizzle in ice water until silky smooth.
Spread on a plate, drizzle olive oil, sprinkle paprika and whole chickpeas.
Serve with warm pita bread.
tips: Process tahini and lemon first before adding chickpeas for the smoothest result.
Ice water whipped in makes the hummus light and fluffy.

name: Tabbouleh
description: Fresh Lebanese parsley salad with bulgur wheat, tomatoes, mint, lemon, and olive oil.
instructions: Soak bulgur in water for 15 minutes; drain and squeeze dry.
Finely chop parsley (stems and all). This should be mostly parsley — not a grain salad.
Finely dice tomatoes, chop mint, and slice spring onions.
Combine parsley, bulgur, tomatoes, mint, and spring onion.
Dress with lemon juice, olive oil, salt, and pepper. Toss well.
Serve at room temperature or chilled, with romaine lettuce leaves for scooping.
tips: Authentic tabbouleh is mostly parsley with a little bulgur — not the other way around.
Use a sharp knife for parsley — a dull knife bruises the leaves and turns them dark.

name: Baba Ganoush
description: Smoky roasted eggplant dip with tahini, garlic, lemon juice, and olive oil.
instructions: Char eggplants directly over a gas flame or under a broiler until skin is blackened and flesh is soft.
Place in a bowl, cover with plastic wrap, and steam 10 minutes.
Peel off charred skin, drain excess liquid from flesh.
Mash eggplant with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and salt (keep it chunky).
Spread on a plate, drizzle olive oil, sprinkle paprika and pomegranate seeds.
Serve with warm pita bread.
tips: The smoky char on the skin is what gives baba ganoush its signature flavor.
Drain the eggplant well — excess water makes it watery.

name: Fattoush
description: Lebanese bread salad with crispy pita chips, fresh vegetables, herbs, and sumac dressing.
instructions: Toast or fry pita bread pieces until golden and crispy.
Chop lettuce, dice cucumber and tomatoes, slice radishes and spring onions.
Chop mint and parsley.
Make dressing: whisk olive oil, lemon juice, sumac, pomegranate molasses, and salt.
Combine vegetables and herbs, toss with dressing.
Add crispy pita chips just before serving — they should be crunchy, not soggy.
tips: Add the pita at the very last moment — the contrast between crispy bread and fresh vegetables is essential.
Sumac is the defining spice of fattoush — do not substitute.

name: Grilled Halloumi Salad
description: Warm grilled halloumi cheese on a bed of mixed greens with roasted vegetables and pomegranate.
instructions: Slice halloumi into 1cm thick slices. Pat dry.
Grill or pan-fry halloumi in a dry or lightly oiled pan until golden, about 2 minutes per side.
Arrange mixed greens on a platter with diced cucumber, halved tomatoes, sliced onion, and pepper.
Place warm halloumi on top.
Dress with olive oil, lemon juice, oregano, and salt.
Scatter pomegranate seeds and serve immediately while halloumi is warm.
tips: Halloumi must be served warm — it becomes rubbery as it cools.
A high-heat, dry pan gives the best golden crust.

name: Spanakopita
description: Flaky Greek phyllo pastry filled with spinach, feta, dill, and onion.
instructions: Wilt spinach in a pan, squeeze out all water, and chop finely.
Sauté diced onion until soft. Mix with spinach, crumbled feta, eggs, chopped dill, spring onion, nutmeg, salt, and pepper.
Melt butter with olive oil. Layer 6 phyllo sheets in a baking dish, brushing each with butter-oil mix.
Spread spinach filling evenly. Top with 6 more phyllo sheets, each brushed with butter-oil.
Score the top layers into squares or diamonds.
Bake at 180C for 35-40 minutes until deeply golden and flaky.
tips: Squeeze every drop of water from the spinach — wet filling makes soggy pastry.
Score only the top layers before baking so it is easy to cut after.

name: Lemon Herb Salmon
description: Baked salmon fillets with lemon, garlic, dill, and olive oil served with roasted vegetables.
instructions: Place salmon on a parchment-lined baking sheet with asparagus and tomatoes around it.
Drizzle olive oil, squeeze lemon juice, scatter minced garlic and dill over everything.
Season with salt and pepper. Lay lemon slices on top of salmon.
Bake at 200C for 12-15 minutes until salmon is just cooked (slightly translucent in the center).
The vegetables should be tender and slightly charred.
Serve immediately with the pan juices spooned over.
tips: Do not overcook salmon — it should be just cooked through, slightly pink in the center.
Skin-on salmon holds together better during baking.

name: Chicken Souvlaki
description: Greek marinated chicken skewers grilled and served in pita with tzatziki, tomato, and onion.
instructions: Cut chicken into 3cm cubes. Marinate in olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, oregano, salt, and pepper for 1 hour.
Thread onto skewers.
Make tzatziki: grate cucumber, squeeze dry, mix with yogurt, garlic, lemon juice, and salt.
Grill skewers over high heat, turning, until charred and cooked through, about 10 minutes.
Warm pita bread on the grill.
Serve chicken in pita with tzatziki, sliced tomato, and red onion.
tips: Marinate at least 1 hour — overnight is even better.
Do not over-pack the skewers — leave small gaps for even cooking.

name: Lamb Kofta
description: Spiced ground lamb skewers grilled until charred, served with flatbread and yogurt sauce.
instructions: Grate onion and squeeze out moisture. Mix with lamb, minced garlic, chopped parsley, and all spices.
Knead mixture vigorously for 2 minutes until sticky and well combined.
Shape into oval logs around skewers, or form into small sausage shapes.
Grill over high heat, turning, for 8-10 minutes until charred outside and cooked through.
Mix yogurt with lemon juice and salt for a dipping sauce.
Serve with warm flatbread, yogurt sauce, and a green salad.
tips: Kneading the meat develops myosin, which binds it to the skewer.
If the mixture is too soft, refrigerate 30 minutes before shaping.

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves (Dolma)
description: Grape leaves stuffed with herbed rice, pine nuts, and currants, braised in olive oil and lemon.
instructions: Sauté diced onion in 2 tbsp oil until soft. Add rice, pine nuts, currants, allspice, cinnamon; stir 2 minutes.
Add chopped mint and dill, salt, and 200ml water. Cook 5 minutes until water is absorbed. Cool.
Rinse grape leaves if salted. Place a leaf shiny-side down, add 1 tbsp filling near the stem.
Fold sides in, roll up tightly.
Pack rolls snugly in a pot seam-side down. Add remaining oil, lemon juice, and enough water to just cover.
Place a plate on top as a weight. Simmer 45 minutes until rice is tender. Serve at room temperature.
tips: Pack the dolmas very tightly in the pot — they should not move during cooking.
These are best served at room temperature or cold, not hot.

name: Ratatouille
description: Provencal vegetable stew of eggplant, zucchini, bell peppers, and tomatoes with herbs.
instructions: Dice eggplant, zucchini, bell peppers, and onion into 2cm pieces.
Sauté each vegetable separately in olive oil until lightly golden; set aside.
Cook garlic in the same pan, add canned tomatoes and thyme, simmer 10 minutes.
Combine all sautéed vegetables with the tomato sauce. Season with salt and pepper.
Cover and simmer gently for 20 minutes until vegetables are tender but still hold their shape.
Garnish with fresh basil and a drizzle of olive oil. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Cooking vegetables separately prevents them from becoming a mushy stew.
Ratatouille tastes better the next day as flavors meld.

name: Caprese Salad
description: Simple Italian salad of fresh mozzarella, ripe tomatoes, basil, olive oil, and balsamic glaze.
instructions: Slice mozzarella and tomatoes into 1cm thick rounds.
Arrange alternating slices of tomato and mozzarella on a platter, overlapping slightly.
Tuck fresh basil leaves between the slices.
Drizzle generously with olive oil and balsamic glaze.
Season with flaky sea salt and cracked black pepper.
Serve immediately at room temperature.
tips: Use the best quality ingredients you can find — there is nowhere to hide in this simple dish.
Tomatoes must be ripe and at room temperature, not cold from the fridge.
